Original problem: run_study's finally block called tracker.end_run(run_id) on every exit, and MlflowExperimentTracker.end_run called set_terminated(run_id) with no status, so MLflow defaulted to FINISHED for both success and abort, making failed runs indistinguishable. Any phase that raises leaves ok=False -> the run is terminated FAILED. I confirmed the installed mlflow's set_terminated signature accepts (run_id, status, ...), so the status threads through to the real backend. Complete: a dedicated regression test forces a GateError abort and asserts the tracker ended with status \"FAILED\", and the happy-path test asserts \"FINISHED\"; both would catch a regression (e.g. reverting to status-less end_run or losing the ok flag). Original problem: run_study's finally block called tracker.end_run(run_id) on every exit, and MlflowExperimentTracker.end_run called set_terminated(run_id) with no status, so MLflow defaulted to FINISHED for both success and abort, making failed runs indistinguishable. This is a sound, non-hacky approach and it does meaningfully tame the worst case the finding called out: the rate-limit backoff storm no longer multiplies across all outer iterations. With a realistic advancing clock the deadline short-circuits subsequent re-rolls, so the total is bounded to roughly one inner exhaustion instead of (MAX_RETRIES+1)x. There is a regression test (test_run_json_overall_deadline_bounds_rerolls) and all 9 run_json/retry/saturation tests pass against the venv. Parse failures and transient/rate failures are indistinguishable to the outer loop because both surface as ReasoningError.\n\n2) The \"absolute wall-clock deadline\" does NOT actually bound a single call's blocking time. The deadline is only checked at the top of each outer re-roll (line 585: `if attempt > 0 and time.monotonic() >= deadline`), never DURING the inner _run_with_retries backoff loop. PoC: with RUN_JSON_DEADLINE=5.0s and an advancing clock, a single inner rate-limit exhaustion still blocked 450s (=sum(30*(r+1) for r in 0..4)) — 90x the requested deadline — because the inner loop runs to completion before the deadline is ever re-checked. So the deadline caps the NUMBER of re-rolls but not the wall-clock time of any one of them, which is exactly the \"unbounded blocking time\" the HIGH finding is about.\n\nThe regression test only asserts the between-re-roll short-circuit (deadline=0.0 stops after 1 call); it does NOT cover the transient-multiplication count (still 9) nor the case where a single inner loop overruns the deadline. A complete fix would either call _attempt+extract_json directly in run_json's loop (option a) or distinguish parse failures from transient failures so transient errors don't trigger a fresh full inner budget, AND thread the deadline into _run_with_retries (or pass a per-attempt time budget) so a single call's blocking is actually capped.","evidence":"src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:584-600 (run_json re-roll loop still calls _run_with_retries and catches all ReasoningError); seam.py:585 (deadline checked only between re-rolls); seam.py:493-511 (_run_with_retries inner transient+rate budgets, unbounded by deadline); seam.py:272-280 (extract_json raises plain ReasoningError, indistinguishable from transient). It adds self._lock = threading.Lock() in __init__ and refactors record() so the public method acquires the lock (with self._lock:) and delegates the entire read-compute-append to a new _record_locked() helper. The lock is held across the whole sequence — seq = len(self._entries), prev_hash read, clock call, digest, freeze, and self._entries.append(entry) — so two concurrent record() calls can no longer read the same seq/prev_hash and corrupt the chain. This is exactly the suggested_fix (\"hold the lock for the whole method to keep seq/prev_hash/entry_hash mutually consistent\"), not a hack or over-claim, and the docstring update to \"Thread-safe\" is accurate. I verified behaviorally with a PoC against the venv (16 threads x 200 records = 3200 entries): seqs are monotonic and complete (range(3200)) and trail.verify() passes with no chain break. So appropriate=true.\n\ncomplete=false: there is NO regression test for this finding. git diff main --name-only -- tests/test_provenance.py returns nothing — the file is identical to main. Critically, it also fixes a deeper subtlety the original finding only partially raised: it does NOT merely OMIT unsafe keys — it OVERWRITES each to \"\". This is the correct insight because the claude-agent-sdk builds the child env as {**os.environ, **options.env}, so an omitted key would fall through to its inherited value and the redirect/credential would survive. The subscription CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN (no ANTHROPIC_ prefix, not matched) is preserved so auth still works. The call site passes env=scrubbed_env() directly, so the hardening is actually applied.\n\nThe denylist (vs. a strict allowlist) is the explicitly-offered alternative in suggested_fix and is reasonable here; it is not a hack or over-claim — the docstrings honestly describe the merge-overwrite rationale.\n\nThe regression test is strong: test_scrubbed_env_overwrites_unsafe_and_survives_sdk_merge simulates the exact SDK merge ({**os.environ, **scrubbed}) and asserts ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL, CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK, HTTPS_PROXY, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY all resolve to \"\" in the child while CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN survives. The old code materialized the full `points` list (one PointStruct per id, each holding `list(v)`) before the chunking loop. The new code iterates `range(0, len(ids), batch_size)` and builds the PointStruct list ONE BATCH AT A TIME inside the loop, sending it and letting it be discarded before the next batch. Only one batch of PointStructs (batch_size items) exists in memory at any moment, exactly matching the suggested fix. It also adds a sensible `batch_size < 1` ValueError guard and preserves the length-mismatch check. So `appropriate` = true.\n\nHowever, `complete` = false because the regression test does NOT actually catch a regression of the memory behavior it is supposed to guard. The test (tests/test_persistence.py::test_vector_store_upsert_streams_batches_and_owns_client) injects a FakeClient and asserts `fc.batches == [2, 2, 1]` for 5 ids with batch_size=2. But the OLD (buggy) code would produce the identical sent-batch sizes [2, 2, 1] — it sliced the fully-materialized list into the same chunks (points[0:2], [2:4], [4:6]). The test only observes the lengths of payloads sent to the client; it does not spy on WHEN/HOW MANY PointStructs are constructed (no patch/count of PointStruct, no interleaving check, no peak-memory assertion). Therefore a revert to up-front materialization would still pass this test, so it would not catch a regression of the actual memory-bounding fix (#118).
